refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-actuator round-trip) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is vague on measurement point (end-to-end vs. link-only), percentile definition, and network assumptions. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured from sensor acquisition through network transmission to actuator command execution, with explicit p95 acceptance criterion and conditional network scope. This precision is critical for safety-critical teleoperation where operator reaction time and equipment response predictability directly impact worksite safety.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrumented teleoperation test harness measuring timestamp deltas at sensor input and actuator output across representative network conditions (latency, jitter, packet loss profiles). Collect 1000+ round-trip samples; compute p95 latency distribution. Validate under both nominal and degraded network scenarios per agreed network conditions specification.
sub_frs: FR-TELE-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, load, video) with minimal capture-to-transmission latency.
FR-TELE-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with bounded network latency.
FR-TELE-003: Receive operator tasking commands at remote site with bounded network latency.
FR-TELE-004: Execute tasking commands on vehicle actuators with minimal command-to-response latency.
FR-TELE-005: Monitor and report teleoperation latency health; degrade or halt operation if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Distributed edge-compute architecture with local sensor buffering, predictive command queuing, and adaptive codec selection to minimize end-to-end latency variance.
DP-B: Centralized cloud-based teleoperation relay with optimized network routing, packet prioritization, and latency compensation via operator-side prediction.

DP-C: Hybrid edge-cloud architecture with local real-time sensor-to-actuator loop (hard real-time, <50 ms) and remote operator supervisory control (soft real-time, <150 ms p95) via dual-channel network with fallback.
DP-D: Direct point-to-point teleoperation with minimal intermediaries, local network optimization, and operator-side latency buffering via predictive UI.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid edge-cloud architecture with local real-time sensor-to-actuator loop (hard real-time, <50 ms) and remote operator supervisory control (soft real-time, <150 ms p95) via dual-channel network with fallback.

potential_sources: Network infrastructure design (dual-channel provisioning) couples to external telecom provider SLAs; mitigation: contractual latency guarantees and fallback to lower-bandwidth channel.
Sensor acquisition rate and codec selection couple to vehicle compute platform; mitigation: standardized sensor interface and pluggable codec library.
Operator UI responsiveness couples to remote workstation capability; mitigation: operator-side latency compensation via predictive rendering.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C decouples the hard real-time vehicle control loop from soft real-time remote supervision, reducing coupling complexity. Local loop ensures safety even if remote link degrades. Dual-channel network introduces infrastructure dependency but provides resilience.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network infrastructure supports dual-channel provisioning (primary + fallback) with <150 ms p95 latency under agreed conditions.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle compute platform can sustain local real-time loop at <50 ms cycle time with sensor buffering.
ASSUMPTION: Operator workstation supports predictive UI rendering to mask residual network latency.
RISK: If network provider cannot guarantee p95 ≤ 150 ms, teleoperation acceptance criterion cannot be met; mitigation: early network SLA validation.
RISK: If vehicle compute platform lacks sufficient real-time capability, local loop cannot be guaranteed; mitigation: hardware-in-loop testing early in design.
RISK: Operator training required for predictive UI; poor training increases accident risk; mitigation: comprehensive operator certification program.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Explicitly define 'agreed network conditions' in a separate Network SLA specification document. Quantify sensor acquisition latency budget (e.g., <10 ms), network transmission budget (e.g., <100 ms), and actuator response budget (e.g., <40 ms) to enable allocation to sub-systems. Add fallback mode specification: behavior when p95 latency exceeds 150 ms (e.g., autonomous hold, operator alert, graceful shutdown).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic: sensor data acquisition and buffering, network transmission/reception, operator command parsing, actuator command generation, latency monitoring, and fallback mode management. Owns the hybrid edge-cloud architecture and dual-channel network coordination. Interfaces with Vehicle for sensor/actuator access and with Safety Services for latency health reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module emits sensor telemetry (position, orientation, load, video, latency metrics) to remote operator via Network Link. Includes timestamp for latency measurement.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator tasking commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from remote operator via Network Link. Includes command timestamp for latency tracking.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module emits SafetyAlert to Safety Services when latency exceeds safe thresholds or network link degrades, triggering fallback mode or operator notification.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link (dual-channel, primary + fallback) for bidirectional communication with remote operator. Manages channel selection, failover, and latency monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution; other subsystems (Vehicle, Safety Services, Maintenance) are peers. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse teleoperation logic across multiple child modules and obscure ownership. Not recommended.

Vehicle module owns sensor/actuator hardware and local control loops. Teleoperation logic (remote operator interface, network protocol, latency compensation) is orthogonal to vehicle mechanics. Assigning IM to Vehicle would couple remote operation concerns with vehicle design, reducing modularity. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for this FR. Owns sensor buffering, network transmission, command parsing, latency monitoring, and fallback logic. Interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (via Telemetry/Tasking) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert). Minimal diffusion; high cohesion. RECOMMENDED.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts. Teleoperation latency monitoring is a safety concern but is operationally distinct from safety policy logic. Assigning IM to Safety Services would blur the boundary between latency management (operational) and safety enforcement (policy). Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of CR-NGHE-008 because it encapsulates the end-to-end latency management logic: sensor acquisition, network transmission, command reception, actuator execution, and latency monitoring. The module has clear interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout) and minimal coupling to other subsystems. Ownership is unambiguous.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle module does not expose sensor/actuator interfaces with sufficient timing guarantees, Teleoperation cannot achieve <50 ms local loop latency. Mitigation: Vehicle module must document sensor acquisition latency and actuator response latency as part of its interface contract.

RISK: If Network Link interface does not support dual-channel failover with <150 ms p95 latency, Teleoperation cannot meet acceptance criterion. Mitigation: Network Link interface specification must include latency SLA and failover behavior.

RISK: If Safety Services does not respond to SafetyAlert in time to halt operation before unsafe latency occurs, safety goal is not met. Mitigation: Safety Services must define maximum response time for latency-triggered alerts.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the Telemetry, Tasking, and Network Link interface contracts with latency budgets and timing guarantees. Create a separate Network SLA specification document that defines 'agreed network conditions' and dual-channel failover behavior. Ensure Vehicle module documents sensor/actuator latency contributions. Ensure Safety Services defines response time for latency-triggered SafetyAlert.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (end-to-end latency p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions) is directly addressed by DP-C (hybrid edge-cloud with local <50 ms loop and remote <150 ms p95 supervision). The DP logically solves the FR by decomposing latency into manageable budgets (sensor <10 ms, network <100 ms, actuator <40 ms) and providing fallback resilience. No logical gap detected.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle module's sensor/actuator latency performance. If Vehicle does not meet its latency budget, Teleoperation cannot achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms. Mitigation: Vehicle module must be designed concurrently with latency constraints.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the clear owner of DP-C. The module boundary is well-defined: it owns sensor buffering, network transmission, command parsing, actuator execution, and latency monitoring. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clean and minimal. No diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumented teleoperation test harness measuring end-to-end latency across representative network conditions. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with Vehicle and Network Link mocked) or integrated with Vehicle and real network. Acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency requirement. Sub-allocations to Vehicle (sensor/actuator latency), Network Link (transmission latency), and Safety Services (fallback response time) are explicit and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Create a detailed latency budget allocation document: sensor acquisition <10 ms, network transmission <100 ms, actuator response <40 ms. Assign ownership of each budget to Vehicle, Network Link, and Teleoperation respectively.
Define 'agreed network conditions' in a separate Network SLA specification: latency distribution, jitter, packet loss, failover behavior, and dual-channel provisioning.
Add a fallback mode specification: behavior when p95 latency exceeds 150 ms (e.g., autonomous hold, operator alert, graceful shutdown). Assign ownership to Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Ensure Vehicle module documents sensor acquisition latency and actuator response latency as part of its interface contract.
Ensure Safety Services defines maximum response time for latency-triggered SafetyAlert.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-defined, the selected DP is sound and addresses the FR logically, and the assigned IM (Teleoperation) is the clear owner with minimal diffusion. All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass. However, the FR is not atomic: it decomposes into five sub-FRs (sensor acquisition, telemetry transmission, tasking reception, actuator execution, latency monitoring). To ensure complete coverage and traceability, we must decompose to the next level and refine each sub-FR with its own DP and IM assignment. This is a normal and healthy progression in functional decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TELE-001: Acquire sensor data (position, orientation, load, video) with minimal capture-to-transmission latency (<10 ms).
FR-TELE-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with bounded network latency (<100 ms p95).
FR-TELE-003: Receive operator tasking commands at remote site with bounded network latency (<100 ms p95).
FR-TELE-004: Execute tasking commands on vehicle actuators with minimal command-to-response latency (<40 ms).
FR-TELE-005: Monitor and report teleoperation latency health; degrade or halt operation if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports dual-channel provisioning (primary + fallback) with <150 ms p95 latency under agreed conditions.
Vehicle compute platform can sustain local real-time loop at <50 ms cycle time with sensor buffering.
Operator workstation supports predictive UI rendering to mask residual network latency.
Safety Services can respond to latency-triggered SafetyAlert within acceptable time window (TBD).
Sensor acquisition latency is <10 ms; network transmission latency is <100 ms p95; actuator response latency is <40 ms (budgets to be validated).
open_risks: Network provider may not guarantee p95 ≤ 150 ms; early SLA validation required.
Vehicle compute platform may lack sufficient real-time capability; hardware-in-loop testing required early.
Operator training and certification required for predictive UI; poor training increases accident risk.
Fallback mode behavior (autonomous hold, operator alert, graceful shutdown) not yet specified; safety analysis required.
Latency monitoring and health reporting mechanism not yet designed; may introduce additional latency if not carefully implemented.
evidence_needed: Network SLA specification with dual-channel failover behavior and latency guarantees.
Vehicle module interface contract with sensor acquisition and actuator response latency specifications.
Safety Services interface contract with maximum response time for latency-triggered SafetyAlert.
Latency budget allocation document with ownership assignments.
Fallback mode specification and safety analysis.
Instrumented teleoperation test harness and test results validating p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions.
Operator training and certification program for predictive UI.
